revision.comment: Adding [[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)

== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)

Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "諏訪氏}}" to "諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}"
revision.text:
'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives)
{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Goronin}} [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Suwa clan]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:1530 births]] [[Category:1555 deaths]]

== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|武田信玄}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|武田信玄 (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|武田信玄 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})

'''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of {{ill|Takeda Shingen|ja|Takeda Shingen}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|ja|甲陽軍鑑}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Neene|ja|禰々}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|ja|武田信虎}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|ja|躑躅ヶ崎館}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|ja|新田次郎}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|ja|井上靖}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-kō Festival|ja|信玄公祭り}} in {{ill|Kōfu, Yamanashi|ja|甲府市}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (movie)|ja|風林火山 (映画)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|ja|佐久間良子}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yōko Minamino|ja|南野陽子}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|ja|風林火山 (NHK大河ドラマ)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Kō Shibamoto|ja|柴本幸}})
