Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|qq=|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

<!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Takeda Shingen]] [[Category:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[Category:People of Kai Province]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:سووا گوریونین]] [[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{wikidata link|Q8193299}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|qq=|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|qq=|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

<!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Takeda Shingen]] [[Category:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[Category:People of Kai Province]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:سووا گوریونین]] [[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{wikidata link|Q8193299}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives) ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

<!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Takeda Shingen]] [[Category:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[Category:People of Kai Province]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:سووا گوریونین]] [[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{wikidata link|Q8193299}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

<!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Takeda Shingen]] [[Category:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[Category:People of Kai Province]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:سووا گوریونین]] [[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{wikidata link|Q8193299}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives) ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

<!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--><!--Suwa Goronin--> [[Category:People of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Women of the Sengoku period]] [[Category:Takeda Shingen]] [[Category:Takeda Katsuyori]] [[Category:People of Kai Province]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:سووا گوریونین]] [[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{wikidata link|Q8193299}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. She was the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], the head of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''[[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kōyō Gunkan]]'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]]. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father [[:en:Takeda Nobutora|Takeda Nobutora]], Yorishige aligned with the [[:en:Uesugi clan|Uesugi clan]], violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the [[:en:Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki Mansion]].<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])

This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.

Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]]. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya, Nagano]], but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as [[:en:Jirō Nitta|Jirō Nitta]]'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual [[:en:Shingen-ko Festival|Shingen-kō Festival]] in [[:en:Kōfu|Kōfu, Yamanashi]] features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''[[:en:Samurai Banners|Fūrin Kazan (movie)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: [[:en:Yoshiko Sakuma|Yoshiko Sakuma]]) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yoko Minamino|Yōko Minamino]]) * ''[[:en:Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)]]'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: [[:en:Yuki Shibamoto|Kō Shibamoto]])
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives) ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|en|Takeda Nobutora|WD=Q1192628|lt=Takeda Nobutora}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|en|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|WD=Q11637200|lt=Tsutsujigasaki Mansion}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jirō Nitta}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-ko Festival|en|Shingen-ko Festival|WD=Q3562464|lt=Shingen-kō Festival}} in {{ill|Kōfu|en|Kōfu|WD=Q201932|lt=Kōfu, Yamanashi}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Samurai Banners|en|Samurai Banners|WD=Q2712195|lt=Fūrin Kazan (movie)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|en|Yoshiko Sakuma|WD=Q11382739|lt=Yoshiko Sakuma}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yoko Minamino|en|Yoko Minamino|WD=Q456207|lt=Yōko Minamino}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|en|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|WD=Q843000|lt=Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yuki Shibamoto|en|Yuki Shibamoto|WD=Q9036475|lt=Kō Shibamoto}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q8193299]] Wikidata name: Suwa Goryōnin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|en|Takeda Nobutora|WD=Q1192628|lt=Takeda Nobutora}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|en|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|WD=Q11637200|lt=Tsutsujigasaki Mansion}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jirō Nitta}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-ko Festival|en|Shingen-ko Festival|WD=Q3562464|lt=Shingen-kō Festival}} in {{ill|Kōfu|en|Kōfu|WD=Q201932|lt=Kōfu, Yamanashi}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Samurai Banners|en|Samurai Banners|WD=Q2712195|lt=Fūrin Kazan (movie)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|en|Yoshiko Sakuma|WD=Q11382739|lt=Yoshiko Sakuma}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yoko Minamino|en|Yoko Minamino|WD=Q456207|lt=Yōko Minamino}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|en|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|WD=Q843000|lt=Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yuki Shibamoto|en|Yuki Shibamoto|WD=Q9036475|lt=Kō Shibamoto}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives) ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}
{{translated page|ja|諏訪御料人|version=100263054|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jirō Nitta}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-ko Festival|en|Shingen-ko Festival|WD=Q3562464|lt=Shingen-kō Festival}} in {{ill|Kōfu|en|Kōfu|WD=Q201932|lt=Kōfu, Yamanashi}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Samurai Banners|en|Samurai Banners|WD=Q2712195|lt=Fūrin Kazan (movie)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|en|Yoshiko Sakuma|WD=Q11382739|lt=Yoshiko Sakuma}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yoko Minamino|en|Yoko Minamino|WD=Q456207|lt=Yōko Minamino}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|en|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|WD=Q843000|lt=Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yuki Shibamoto|en|Yuki Shibamoto|WD=Q9036475|lt=Kō Shibamoto}})

== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650|1=Suwa}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179|1=Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975|1=Chōkyū}}}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige|1=Suwa Yorishige}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan|1=Kōyō Gunkan}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|en|Takeda Nobutora|WD=Q1192628|lt=Takeda Nobutora|1=Takeda Nobutora}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|en|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|WD=Q11637200|lt=Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|1=Tsutsujigasaki Castle}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano|1=Ina, Nagano}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jirō Nitta|1=Jirō Nitta}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue|1=Yasushi Inoue}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-ko Festival|en|Shingen-ko Festival|WD=Q3562464|lt=Shingen-kō Festival|1=Shingen-ko Festival}} in {{ill|Kōfu|en|Kōfu|WD=Q201932|lt=Kōfu, Yamanashi|1=Kōfu}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Samurai Banners|en|Samurai Banners|WD=Q2712195|lt=Fūrin Kazan (movie)|1=Samurai Banners}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|en|Yoshiko Sakuma|WD=Q11382739|lt=Yoshiko Sakuma|1=Yoshiko Sakuma}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yoko Minamino|en|Yoko Minamino|WD=Q456207|lt=Yōko Minamino|1=Yoko Minamino}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|en|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|WD=Q843000|lt=Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|1=Fūrin Kazan (TV series)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yuki Shibamoto|en|Yuki Shibamoto|WD=Q9036475|lt=Kō Shibamoto|1=Yuki Shibamoto}})
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shunroku Shibazutsu|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}} * {{Cite book|author=Masahiro Hirayama|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014}} * {{Cite book|author=Kazushige Marushima|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|year=2015}}
== External Links == * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004180009_00000 Suwa Taisha, Uehara Castle Ruins, and Ryūkōzan Kannon-in: The Hometown of Yūbu-hime] (NHK Archives) ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:سووا گوریونین]][[ja:諏訪御料人]][[zh:諏訪御料人]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1530|death_date={{death date|1555|12|18}}|birth_place={{ill|Suwa|WD=Q63994650|1=Suwa}}|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}}|children={{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}|father={{ill|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q2511179|1=Suwa Yorishige (daimyo)}}|relatives={{ill|Chōkyū|WD=Q11651975|1=Chōkyū}}}} '''Suwa Gorōnin''' (諏訪御料人, 1530 – December 18, 1555) was a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Takeda Harunobu) and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}. She was the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige|1=Suwa Yorishige}}, the head of the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan|1=Suwa clan}}, and his concubine from the {{ill|Omi clan (麻績氏)|ja|小見氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi clan (麻績氏)|qq=}} (Omishi). Known also as "Taikata-sama," her actual name remains unknown. In historical sources, she is referred to as "Suwa Gorōnin" or "Suwa Gozen," both terms indicating a noblewoman.
== Biography == Suwa Gorōnin's birth year is uncertain, but according to the ''{{ill|Kōyō Gunkan|en|Kōyō Gunkan|WD=Q586111|lt=Kōyō Gunkan|1=Kōyō Gunkan}}'', she became a concubine of Takeda Shingen at age 14 in 1545, suggesting she was born in 1530.<ref name="hira23">{{Cite book|author=平山優|title=長篠合戦と武田勝頼 敗者の日本史9|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|page=23}}</ref><ref name="shiba476">{{Cite book|author=柴辻俊六|title=武田氏家臣団人名辞典|publisher=東京堂出版|year=2015|page=476}}</ref> ''Kōyō Gunkan'' praises her beauty, calling her a "notable beauty."<ref name="shiba476" />
At the time of her father Suwa Yorishige's leadership, the Suwa clan was allied with the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}}. Yorishige married {{ill|Nene Goryōnin|lt=Nene Goryōnin|lt=Neene|ja|禰々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Neene|qq=}}, Takeda Shingen's half-sister, to strengthen the alliance. However, in 1541, after Shingen ousted his father {{ill|Takeda Nobutora|en|Takeda Nobutora|WD=Q1192628|lt=Takeda Nobutora|1=Takeda Nobutora}}, Yorishige aligned with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, violating the Takeda-Suwa alliance. This led to Takeda's invasion of Suwa, resulting in the fall of the Suwa clan and Yorishige's forced seppuku.
To consolidate his control, Shingen took Suwa Gorōnin as a concubine and planned to have their son, Katsuyori, inherit the Suwa clan's legacy.<ref name="hira23" /> She is believed to have entered the Takeda household around 1545, residing at the {{ill|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|en|Tsutsujigasaki Castle|WD=Q11637200|lt=Tsutsujigasaki Mansion|1=Tsutsujigasaki Castle}}.<ref name="shiba476" />
Suwa Gorōnin died on November 6, 1555. Her grave is located at {{ill|Kenpuku-ji|ja|建福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpuku-ji|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano|1=Ina, Nagano}}. Her Buddhist name was recorded as "Kenpukuji-dono Baigan Myōkō Daizenni."<ref name="shiba476" /> Another memorial exists at {{ill|Kannon-in (Okaya City)|ja|観音院 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon-in (Okaya City)|qq=}} in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, but it was constructed after her death based on fictional accounts.
== Influence and Legacy == According to ''{{ill|Tetsuzan-shū|ja|鉄山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''Butsugan Zenji Goroku,'' her son Katsuyori performed rituals in her memory, including the 17th death anniversary ceremony in 1571.<ref name="shiba476" />
There is speculation that she was adopted by {{ill|Netsu Motonao|ja|禰津元直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Netsu Motonao|qq=}} to avoid her identity as a former enemy's daughter. This adoption would mean Katsuyori and {{ill|Takeda Nobukiyo|ja|武田信清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Nobukiyo|1=Takeda Nobukiyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6639853}} were both her children, suggesting she lived until the 1560s.
== Cultural Impact == Suwa Gorōnin is often depicted as Takeda Shingen's favorite concubine, although this characterization is debated among historians. Historical dramas and novels have contributed to this portrayal, such as {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jirō Nitta|1=Jirō Nitta}}'s ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja|Takeda Shingen (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (novel)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', where she is called "Koi-hime," and {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue|1=Yasushi Inoue}}'s ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}'', where she is named "Yūbu-hime."
The annual {{ill|Shingen-ko Festival|en|Shingen-ko Festival|WD=Q3562464|lt=Shingen-kō Festival|1=Shingen-ko Festival}} in {{ill|Kōfu|en|Kōfu|WD=Q201932|lt=Kōfu, Yamanashi|1=Kōfu}} features a "Koi-hime Contest," adding to her legacy in popular culture.
== Depictions in Media == Suwa Gorōnin has been portrayed under various names in films and television dramas: * ''{{ill|Samurai Banners|en|Samurai Banners|WD=Q2712195|lt=Fūrin Kazan (movie)|1=Samurai Banners}}'' as Yūbu-hime (1969, Toho, actress: {{ill|Yoshiko Sakuma|en|Yoshiko Sakuma|WD=Q11382739|lt=Yoshiko Sakuma|1=Yoshiko Sakuma}}) * ''{{ill|Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja|Takeda Shingen (NHK大河ドラマ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen (1988 TV series)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' as Koi-hime (1988, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yoko Minamino|en|Yoko Minamino|WD=Q456207|lt=Yōko Minamino|1=Yoko Minamino}}) * ''{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|en|Fūrin Kazan (TV series)|WD=Q843000|lt=Fūrin Kazan (NHK Taiga Drama)|1=Fūrin Kazan (TV series)}}'' as Yūbu-hime (2007, NHK Taiga Drama, actress: {{ill|Yuki Shibamoto|en|Yuki Shibamoto|WD=Q9036475|lt=Kō Shibamoto|1=Yuki Shibamoto}})
